title: Ordinary Time

infobox.value: ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Tempus per annum*) is the part of the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") in the [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"), which falls outside the two great seasons of [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") and [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide"), or their respective preparatory seasons of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") and [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Ordinary Time thus includes the days between Christmastide and Lent, and between Eastertide and Advent. The [liturgical color](/wiki/Liturgical_color "Liturgical color") assigned to Ordinary Time is [green](/wiki/Green "Green"). The last Sunday of Ordinary Time is the [Solemnity of Christ the King](/wiki/Solemnity_of_Christ_the_King "Solemnity of Christ the King").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Christian liturgical period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/135_funnekotter.jpg/250px-135_funnekotter.jpg)](/wiki/File:135_funnekotter.jpg)

Green is the liturgical colour of Ordinary Time.

| [Liturgical seasons](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") |
| --- |
| * Pre-Christmas   + [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") (*Western*)   + [Nativity Fast](/wiki/Nativity_Fast "Nativity Fast") (*Byzantine*)   + [Annunciation](/wiki/Season_of_Annunciation "Season of Annunciation") (*Syriac*) * [Christmas](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") * [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_season "Epiphany season")   + Ordinary Time (*Western*) * [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent") * [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") (*Western*) / [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") (*Eastern*) * [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum") (*Western*) * [Easter](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide") * [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost_season "Pentecost season")   + Ordinary Time (*Western*) * [Summer](/wiki/Season_of_Summer "Season of Summer") (*East Syriac*) * [Apostles' Fast](/wiki/Apostles%27_Fast "Apostles' Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Dormition Fast](/wiki/Dormition_Fast "Dormition Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Elijah–Cross–Moses](/w/index.php?title=Elijah%E2%80%93Cross%E2%80%93Moses&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elijah–Cross–Moses (page does not exist)") (*East Syriac*) * [Dedication of the Church](/w/index.php?title=Season_of_the_Dedication_of_the_Church&action=edit&redlink=1 "Season of the Dedication of the Church (page does not exist)") (*Syriac*) |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Liturgical_year "Template:Liturgical year") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Liturgical_year "Template talk:Liturgical year") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical_year "Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical year") |

**Ordinary Time** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Tempus per annum*) is the part of the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") in the [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"), which falls outside the two great seasons of [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") and [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide"), or their respective preparatory seasons of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") and [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Ordinary Time thus includes the days between Christmastide and Lent, and between Eastertide and Advent. The [liturgical color](/wiki/Liturgical_color "Liturgical color") assigned to Ordinary Time is [green](/wiki/Green "Green"). The last Sunday of Ordinary Time is the [Solemnity of Christ the King](/wiki/Solemnity_of_Christ_the_King "Solemnity of Christ the King").

Although the word "ordinary" as used here is commonly explained as deriving from the [ordinal numerals](/wiki/Ordinal_numeral "Ordinal numeral") by which the weeks are identified or counted,[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Foley disputes this explanation, noting the lack of ecclesiastical usage and documentary support.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Roman Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_Time&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Roman Rite")]

In the ordinary form of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"), the last day of Christmastide is the Sunday after the [Solemnity of the Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)"), or the Sunday after January 6 in places where the Epiphany is moved to always occur on a Sunday. Ordinary Time begins the following Monday, and the weekdays that follow are reckoned as belonging to the first week of Ordinary Time. The [Feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord") is usually celebrated on the last day of Christmas Time, but if it is displaced to Monday due to the Epiphany being celebrated on January 7 or 8, the Feast of the Baptism falls in Ordinary Time instead.[[5]](#cite_note-UNLY332-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Because Ordinary Time begins on a Monday, there is no day called the "First Sunday in Ordinary Time". Instead, the lowest-numbered Sunday is called the Second Sunday in Ordinary Time. This block of Ordinary Time continues through the Tuesday that immediately precedes [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

Ordinary Time resumes on the Monday after the Solemnity of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"). The two Sundays following Pentecost are the [Solemnity of the Most Holy Trinity](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday") and the [Solemnity of the Most Holy Body and Blood of Christ](/wiki/Feast_of_Corpus_Christi "Feast of Corpus Christi"), which despite being part of Ordinary Time, are not numbered.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) In regional calendars where [Whitmonday](/wiki/Whitmonday "Whitmonday") is a [Day of Obligation](/wiki/Day_of_Obligation "Day of Obligation"), Ordinary Time and the use of the liturgical color green may begin on the following Tuesday.

The last day of Ordinary Time is the day before the [First Sunday of Advent](/wiki/First_Sunday_of_Advent "First Sunday of Advent"). The last Sunday of Ordinary Time is the [Solemnity of Christ the King](/wiki/Feast_of_Christ_the_King "Feast of Christ the King"), with the Sunday before that being the Thirty-Third Sunday in Ordinary Time, with the ordinal numbers counting backwards from that point.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

Due to the configuration of the calendar year, Ordinary Time may have a total of either 33 or 34 weeks. As a [mnemonic](/wiki/Mnemonic "Mnemonic"), if the First Sunday of Advent is in November, the previous liturgical year's Ordinary Time will have 33 weeks. If it falls on December 2 or 3, it will have 34 weeks. However, if it falls on December 1, the previous year's Ordinary Time will have 34 weeks only when it is a leap year.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In a year where Ordinary Time has 33 weeks, the omitted week is the one between the weeks immediately surrounding Lent and Easter Time, which varies with the [date of Easter](/wiki/Date_of_Easter "Date of Easter"). This is because the weeks always count forward from the first week at the beginning of Ordinary Time, and separately backwards from the thirty-fourth week at the end of Ordinary Time.

The decision to treat the whole of Ordinary Time as a unit led to abandonment of the previous terminology, whereby the Sundays of the first period were called Sundays after Epiphany and those of the second period Sundays after Pentecost.

### Solemnities, feasts, and commemorations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_Time&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Solemnities, feasts, and commemorations")]

The celebration of an Ordinary Time weekday gives way to that of any [solemnity](/wiki/Solemnity "Solemnity"), feast, or obligatory [memorial](/wiki/Memorial_(liturgy) "Memorial (liturgy)") that falls on the same day, and may optionally be replaced by that of a non-obligatory memorial or of any saint mentioned in the [Roman Martyrology](/wiki/Roman_Martyrology "Roman Martyrology") for that day.

The solemnities, feasts, and commemorations of the [General Roman Calendar](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar "General Roman Calendar") which may, according to the [Ranking of liturgical days in the Roman Rite](/wiki/Ranking_of_liturgical_days_in_the_Roman_Rite "Ranking of liturgical days in the Roman Rite"), replace a Sunday of Ordinary Time are:[[9]](#cite_note-9)

* [Feast of the Presentation of the Lord](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple") on 2 February
* [Solemnity of the Most Holy Trinity](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday") on the Sunday after Pentecost
* [Solemnity of the Nativity of Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/Nativity_of_St._John_the_Baptist "Nativity of St. John the Baptist") on 24 June
* [Solemnity of Saints Peter and Paul](/wiki/Feast_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul "Feast of Saints Peter and Paul") on 29 June
* [Feast of the Transfiguration of the Lord](/wiki/Transfiguration_of_Jesus "Transfiguration of Jesus") on 6 August
* [Solemnity of the Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") on 15 August
* [Feast of the Exaltation of the Holy Cross](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Cross "Feast of the Cross") on 14 September
* [Solemnity of All Saints](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") on 1 November
* [Commemoration of All the Faithful Departed](/wiki/Commemoration_of_All_the_Faithful_Departed "Commemoration of All the Faithful Departed") on 2 November
* [Feast of the Dedication of the Basilica of Saint John Lateran in Rome](/wiki/Basilica_of_St._John_Lateran "Basilica of St. John Lateran") on 9 November

The Universal Norms on the Liturgical Year and the General Roman Calendar also lists as [proper solemnities](/wiki/Solemnity#Proper_solemnities "Solemnity") (which outrank in the relevant church building or community Sundays in Ordinary Time):

* The Solemnity of the principal patron of the place, city, or state
* The Solemnity of the dedication and the anniversary of the dedication of one's own church
* The Solemnity of the title of one's own church
* The Solemnity either of the title or of the founder or of the principal Patron of an Order or Congregation.

## Revised Common Lectionary usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_Time&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Revised Common Lectionary usage")]

Following the lead of the liturgical reforms of the Roman Rite, many [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") churches also adopted the concept of an *Ordinary Time* alongside the [Revised Common Lectionary](/wiki/Revised_Common_Lectionary "Revised Common Lectionary"), which applies the term to the period between Pentecost and Advent. However, use of the term is not common.

Those that have adopted the Revised Common Lectionary include churches of the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Old Catholic](/wiki/Old_Catholic "Old Catholic") and [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Church "Reformed Church") traditions.[[10]](#cite_note-Holmes2012-10)

Some [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") denominations set off a time at the end of Ordinary Time known as [Kingdomtide](/wiki/Kingdomtide "Kingdomtide") or Season of End Times. This period can range anywhere from only the three Sundays prior to Christ the King (as in the Wisconsin Synod Lutheran) to 13 or 14 weeks (most notably in the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church")). The Church of England observes this time between All Saints and Advent Sunday.

In some traditions, what in the Roman Rite is the first period of Ordinary Time is called [Epiphanytide](/wiki/Epiphanytide "Epiphanytide") (beginning on [Epiphany Day](/wiki/Epiphany_Day "Epiphany Day") in the Anglican Communion and Methodist churches)[[11]](#cite_note-11) and from Trinity Sunday to Advent is called Trinitytide.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the Church of England, Sundays during "Ordinary Time" in this narrower sense are called "Sundays after Trinity", except the final four, which are termed "Sundays before Advent". In the [Episcopal Church (United States)](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), it is normal to refer to Sundays after Epiphany and Sundays after Pentecost (not Trinity).

The total number of Sundays varies according to the date of Easter and can range anything from 18 to 23. When there are 23, the [Collect](/wiki/Collect "Collect") and [Post-Communion](/wiki/Post-Communion "Post-Communion") for the 22nd Sunday are taken from the provision for the Third Sunday before Lent.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_Time&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Feria](/wiki/Feria "Feria")

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordinary_Time&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notes")]

3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** ["The Liturgical Calendar: Ordinary Time is Extraordinary Time"](https://media.ascensionpress.com/podcast/the-liturgical-calendar-ordinary-time-is-extraordinary-time/). *Ascension Press Media*. Retrieved 25 August 2025. "Ordinary Time is called "ordinary" not because it is not special or less significant but simply because the weeks of Ordinary Time are numbered. The Latin word ordinalis, which refers to numbers in a series, stems from the Latin word ordo, from which we get the English word order."
4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Foley, Michael (2019). [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1353/atp.2019.0002](https://doi.org/10.1353%2Fatp.2019.0002).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-UNLY332_5-0)** ["*Universal Norms on the Liturgical Year*, 33"](http://www.liturgyoffice.org.uk/Calendar/Info/GNLY.pdf) (PDF). Retrieved 18 June 2018.

8. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** ["Liturgical Calendar: General Roman Calendar"](https://web.archive.org/web/20221006125758/https://www.gcatholic.org/calendar/2022/General-A-en.htm). Archived from [the original](https://www.gcatholic.org/calendar/2022/General-A-en.htm) on 6 October 2022.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** ["Universal Norms on the Liturgical Year and the General Roman Calendar: Table of liturgical days according to their order of precedence, p. 13"](https://www.liturgyoffice.org.uk/Calendar/Info/GNLY.pdf) (PDF).
10. **[^](#cite_ref-Holmes2012_10-0)** Holmes, Stephen Mark (1 October 2012). *The Fathers on the Sunday Gospels*. Liturgical Press. p. 22. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780814635100](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780814635100 "Special:BookSources/9780814635100"). "The *Revised Common Lectionary* has been subsequently adopted by many English-speaking Protestant denominations such as the Church of Scotland and various Lutheran and Reformed churches. It has also been adopted by some Old Catholic churches and is widely used throughout the Anglican Communion, for example by the Church of Ireland, the Scottish Episcopal Church, the Church in Wales, the Episcopal Church (US) and the Anglican churches of Canada, Australia, Aotearoa/New Zealand and Polynesia, Melanesia, the West Indies, Central Africa, and Southern Africa. In the Church of England the two-year Sunday Lectionary of the *Alternative Service Book 1980* was replaced in 2000 by an adapted version of the *Revised Common Lectionary* in *Common Worship*."
11. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** ["Epiphany"](https://www.bbc.co.uk/religion/religions/christianity/holydays/epiphany.shtml). [BBC Online](/wiki/BBC_Online "BBC Online"). 7 October 2011. Retrieved 5 June 2016. "For many Protestant church traditions, the season of Epiphany extends from 6 January until Ash Wednesday, which begins the season of Lent leading to Easter."
12. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** ["Trinitytide"](http://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Trinitytide). [Merriam-Webster](/wiki/Merriam-Webster "Merriam-Webster"). 5 June 2016. Retrieved 5 June 2016. "Definition of Trinitytide: the season of the church year between Trinity Sunday and Advent"
